358	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
359	  % NOTE (iter-161): the iter-160 SIGNATURE GAP is RESOLVED. The Lean proof wires Step 2
360	  % (morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, PROVEN axiom-clean) over Step 1
361	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
362	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite
363	  % type, so [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom] is now a stated hypothesis of this lemma (and across the
364	  % chain). With it, the `JacobsonSpace U` instance is a routine discharge (LocallyOfFiniteType.
365	  % jacobsonSpace + JacobsonSpace.of_isOpenEmbedding), no longer an as-typed gap. The only `sorry`
366	  % reachable from this lemma is now Step 1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy). The hypothesis
367	  % set is recorded in the statement and proof prose below (both [IsAlgClosed k̄] AND
368	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom]); the earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim
369	  % is retired.
370	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
372	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
373	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
374	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
375	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
376	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
377	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
378	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
379	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
380	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
381	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
382	  consumes), and \(Z\) separated. Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a \(\bar k\)-point
383	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
384	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
385	  endomorphism. Suppose:
386	  \begin{itemize}
387	    \item \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) is a \(p_2\)-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
388	      for a set \(V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y\) (so \(U\) contains the whole fibre \(X_y\) over each of
389	      its points);
390	    \item \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) is an \emph{affine} open;
391	    \item \(f(U) \subseteq U_0\), i.e.\ \(f\) maps \(U\) into the affine \(U_0\).
392	  \end{itemize}
393	  Then \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on \(U\) as scheme morphisms:
394	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\).
395	\end{lemma}
396	\begin{proof}
397	  \leanok
398	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete
399	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
400	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
401	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
402	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
403	  \(H^0\) base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
404	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
405	  \medskip
406	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
407	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).
408	  Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type over \(\bar k\), the residue field
409	  is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Because \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) is saturated, \(U\) contains the
410	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
411	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
412	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and
413	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into
414	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
415	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
416	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
417	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
418	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
419	  \medskip
420	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
421	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
422	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
423	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
424	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
425	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
426	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
427	  \(\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U\), whose range is dense --- and feeds
428	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
429	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
430	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
431	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
432	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
433	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
434	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
435	\end{proof}
436	\begin{lemma}\leanok
437	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
438	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
439	  
440	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
441	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
442	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
443	  Let \(W\), \(Z\) be schemes with \(W\) \emph{reduced}, \(W\) a \emph{Jacobson space}, and \(Z\)
444	  \emph{separated}. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z\) be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
445	  \(x \in W\), the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
446	  \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to W\) (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
447	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\(), then \)g_1 = g_2$.
448	\end{lemma}
449	\begin{proof}
450	\leanok
451	  This is the ``dense closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
452	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
453	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
454	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
455	  dominant probe.
456	  Form the coproduct \(P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)\) and the morphism
457	  \(\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W\) defined by \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\) of the residue-field points
458	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\). Its topological range contains every closed point of \(W\):
459	  the inclusion \(\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P\) composes with \(\mathrm{probe}\) to
460	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) (the defining property of \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\)), and the
461	  range of \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) is the singleton \(\{x\}\)
462	  (\(\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}\)). Because \(W\) is a Jacobson space, the closed points
463	  are dense (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\), i.e.\ \(\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W\)), so
464	  the range of \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dense and \(\mathrm{probe}\) is \emph{dominant}.
465	  Componentwise, \(g_1\) and \(g_2\) agree after \(\mathrm{probe}\): precomposing with each coproduct
466	  inclusion \(\iota_x\) reduces (by \(\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}\)) to
467	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\),
468	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point \(x\). By the universal property of the coproduct
469	  (\(\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}\)) this gives \(\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2\).
470	  Since \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dominant and \(Z\) is separated, \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) cancels it
471	  on the left to yield \(g_1 = g_2\).
472	\end{proof}
473	\begin{lemma}
474	\leanok
475	[Global-sections constancy: two \(\bar k\)-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
476	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
478	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
479	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
480	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
481	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
482	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(W\) be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
483	  morphism \(w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k\) that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
484	  type. Let \(g \colon W \to V\) be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme \(V\). Then any two
485	  \(\bar k\)-points of \(W\) --- i.e.\ any two sections \(a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W\) of \(w\) (so
486	  \(a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\) and \(b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\)) --- satisfy \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\).
487	  \medskip
488	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
489	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) collapses the finite extension \(\Gamma(W)/\bar k\); integrality of
490	  \(W\) makes \(\Gamma(W)\) a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
491	  \(\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}\) is what upgrades \(\Gamma(W)\) to a \emph{field} (without it
492	  \(\mathbb A^1\), with \(\Gamma = \bar k[t]\) and two distinct \(\bar k\)-points, is a counterexample);
493	  \(\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\) gives finiteness of \(\Gamma(W)\) over \(\bar k\); and
494	  \([\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]\) is what lets \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\) pin the morphism by its ring map
495	  on global sections.
496	\end{lemma}
497	\begin{proof}
498	\leanok
499	  Because \(W\) is integral and \(w\) is universally closed, the global sections
500	  \(\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)\) form a field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), and the structure
501	  ring map \(w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)\) (the map \(\mathtt{wk.appTop}\) after the canonical
502	  identification \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k\)) is integral / module-finite
503	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)). Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and
504	  \(\Gamma(W)\) is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
505	  (\(\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}\)); thus \(\Gamma(W) = \bar k\) and
506	  \(w^\sharp\) is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
507	  \(a\), \(b\) left-invert \(w^\sharp\) on global sections (they are sections of \(w\)), so they induce the
508	  \emph{same} ring map \(a^\sharp = b^\sharp\) on \(\Gamma(W)\) (cancel the epimorphism \(w^\sharp\)). A
509	  morphism into the \emph{affine} \(V\) is pinned by its global-sections ring map
510	  (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), whence \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\). This realises, without
511	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
512	  quoted from Mumford above.
513	\end{proof}
514	\begin{lemma}
515	\leanok
516	[Integrality descends to a retract of an integral scheme]
517	  \label{lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract}
519	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) project-bespoke (elementary topology + algebra); NO external source.
520	  % LANDED axiom-clean as `AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract`, stated generically for any
521	  % retract `r : S ⟶ T`, `pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` of an integral `T` (more general than
522	  % the `X`/`X×Y`/`p_1` instance below). PROOF DIVERGENCE (cosmetic, sound either way): the Lean
523	  % proves reducedness PER-STALK (each `S.stalk x` embeds via `pr.stalkMap` into the reduced
524	  % `T.stalk`, then `isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`), not via the global-sections split-injection
525	  % the prose below describes. Plan/blueprint-writer may align the prose to the stalk route.
526	  Let \(X \times Y\) be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
527	  \(\bar k\)). Suppose \(X\) is a \emph{retract} of \(X \times Y\): there is a section
528	  \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) of the first projection \(p_1\), i.e.\ \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\).
529	  Then \(X\) is integral.
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	\leanok
533	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
534	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\) makes the pullback on global
535	  sections \(p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) split
536	  injective (it is left-inverted by \(s^\sharp\)). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
537	  \(\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) embeds \(\mathcal O_X(X)\) into a reduced ring, so it too is
538	  reduced (\(\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}\) on the split injection); hence \(X\) is reduced.
539	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection \(p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X\) is surjective because it
540	  admits the section \(s\) (every point of \(X\) is \(p_1(s(\cdot))\)). The continuous image of an
541	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as \(X \times Y\) is irreducible and \(p_1\) is a
542	  continuous surjection onto \(X\), the space of \(X\) is irreducible.
543	  Reduced and irreducible together give that \(X\) is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
544	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
545	  \(X_y \cong X\) must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
546	\end{proof}
547	\begin{lemma}\leanok
548	[Step~1: per-closed-slice constancy of a proper slice into an affine]
549	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
550	  
551	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
552	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
554	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
555	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
556	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
557	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
558	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
559	  With the data of \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} --- \(\bar k\) algebraically
560	  closed, \(X\) proper over \(\bar k\), \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced, and locally of
561	  finite type, \(Z\) separated, a \(\bar k\)-point \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), a morphism
562	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\), a \(p_2\)-saturated open \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) mapping into an
563	  affine open \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) --- fix a \emph{closed} point \(x\) of \(U\). Then \(f\) and the collapsed
564	  map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) (with $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
565	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\(, i.e.\ \)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\() agree at \)x$
566	  after the residue-field probe \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to U\):
567	  \[ U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi f)
568	     = U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)). \]
569	\end{lemma}

571	  \leanok
572	  This is Mumford's per-slice step, realised \emph{cohomology-free} (route~B): the relative Stein /
573	  proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap and is
574	  deliberately avoided. The closed point \(x\) of \(U\) lies over a closed point
575	  \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\); since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type
576	  over \(\bar k\), the residue field is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Saturation \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
577	  puts the \emph{entire} proper integral fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\) inside \(U\), so \(f\) maps \(X_y\)
578	  into the affine \(U_0\). Because \(X_y\) is proper integral over \(\bar k\), its global sections form a
579	  field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), module-finite over \(\bar k\)

705	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
706	    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
707	    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]

712	  refine ⟨ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ f, ?_⟩

716	        = (1 : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A) := by

754	/-- **Headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-`0` curve.** Over an algebraically closed field
755	`k̄` (arbitrary characteristic — no `[CharZero kbar]`), let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically
756	irreducible curve with `genus C = 0` and `A` an abelian variety (smooth proper geom-irred group
757	scheme). Then every morphism `f : C ⟶ A` killing a `k̄`-point `p` (`p ≫ f = η[A]`) equals the
758	constant morphism at the identity, `f = toUnit C ≫ η[A]`.
759	
760	This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that `genusZeroWitness` consumes
761	(via the `k̄ → k` descent step hosted in `AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian`). Its signature mirrors
762	`AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar` of `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar` **verbatim except**
763	the `[CharZero kbar]` instance is dropped. Combine `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (`C ≅ ℙ¹`) with
764	`morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` (`ℙ¹ → A` constant) and pin the constant value to `η[A]` via
765	the pointed hypothesis. No `df = 0`, no Serre duality, no Picard representability.

769	bridge closes and the helper's product-instance + dominance sorries discharge, lifts to

771	theorem rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme
772	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
773	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
774	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
775	    [IsProper C.hom]
776	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
777	    (_hgenus : genus C = 0)
778	    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
779	    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
780	    (f : C ⟶ A)
781	    (p : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ C)
782	    (_hf : p ≫ f = η[A]) :
783	    f = (toUnit C ≫ η[A]) := by
784	  -- Transport `f` along `C ≅ ProjectiveLineBar kbar` to a morphism `g : ℙ¹ ⟶ A`.
785	  obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus
786	  set g : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A := φ.inv ≫ f with hgdef
787	  -- `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` gives `g = toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ a₀` for some `a₀`.
788	  obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ := morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g
789	  -- Pin `a₀ = η[A]` via the pointed hypothesis on `f`.
790	  have hpoint : (p ≫ φ.hom) ≫ g = η[A] := by
791	    rw [hgdef, Category.assoc, ← Category.assoc φ.hom, φ.hom_inv_id, Category.id_comp]
792	    exact _hf
793	  have hcst : a₀ = η[A] := by
794	    rw [hga₀, ← Category.assoc,
795	      toUnit_unique ((p ≫ φ.hom) ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar)) (𝟙 _),
796	      Category.id_comp] at hpoint
797	    exact hpoint
798	  rw [hcst] at hga₀
799	  -- Back-transport: `f = φ.hom ≫ g = φ.hom ≫ toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ η[A] = toUnit C ≫ η[A]`.
800	  calc f
801	      = φ.hom ≫ φ.inv ≫ f := by
802	        rw [← Category.assoc, φ.hom_inv_id, Category.id_comp]
803	    _ = φ.hom ≫ g := by rw [hgdef]
804	    _ = φ.hom ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫ η[A] := by rw [hga₀]
805	    _ = toUnit C ≫ η[A] := by
806	        rw [← Category.assoc,
807	          toUnit_unique (φ.hom ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar)) (toUnit C)]

687	theorem app_eq_appIso_inv_app_of_comp_eq {X Y U : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ U) (g : U ⟶ X) (fg : Y ⟶ X)
688	    (H : fg = f ≫ g) [h : IsOpenImmersion g] (V : U.Opens) :
689	    f.app V = (g.appIso V).inv ≫ fg.app (g ''ᵁ V) ≫ Y.presheaf.map
690	      (eqToHom <| IsOpenImmersion.app_eq_invApp_app_of_comp_eq_aux f g fg H V).op := by
691	  subst H
692	  rw [Scheme.Hom.comp_app, Category.assoc, Scheme.Hom.appIso_inv_app_assoc, f.naturality_assoc,
693	    ← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp, Quiver.Hom.unop_op, eqToHom_map, eqToHom_trans,
694	    eqToHom_op, eqToHom_refl, CategoryTheory.Functor.map_id, Category.comp_id]
695	
696	theorem lift_app {X Y U : Scheme.{u}} (f : U ⟶ Y) (g : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] (H)
697	    (V : U.Opens) :
698	    (lift f g H).app V = (f.appIso V).inv ≫ g.app (f ''ᵁ V) ≫
699	      X.presheaf.map (eqToHom <| app_eq_invApp_app_of_comp_eq_aux _ _ _ (lift_fac ..).symm V).op :=
700	  IsOpenImmersion.app_eq_appIso_inv_app_of_comp_eq _ _ _ (lift_fac _ _ _).symm _
701	
702	lemma isPullback {U V X Y : Scheme.{u}} (g : U ⟶ V) (iU : U ⟶ X) (iV : V ⟶ Y) (f : X ⟶ Y)
703	    [IsOpenImmersion iU] [IsOpenImmersion iV] (H : iU ≫ f = g ≫ iV)
704	    (H' : f ⁻¹ᵁ iV.opensRange = iU.opensRange) : IsPullback g iU iV f := by
705	  let e := IsOpenImmersion.isoOfRangeEq (pullback.snd iV f) iU
706	    (by simpa [range_pullbackSnd] using congr(($H').1))
707	  convert (IsPullback.of_horiz_isIso (show CommSq e.inv iU (pullback.snd iV f) (𝟙 X) from
708	    ⟨by simp [e]⟩)).paste_horiz (IsPullback.of_hasPullback iV f)
709	  simp [← cancel_mono iV, e, pullback.condition, H]
710	
711	/-- If `f` is an open immersion `X ⟶ Y`, the global sections of `X`
712	are naturally isomorphic to the sections of `Y` over the image of `f`. -/
713	noncomputable
714	def ΓIso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] (U : Y.Opens) :
715	    Γ(X, f ⁻¹ᵁ U) ≅ Γ(Y, f.opensRange ⊓ U) :=
716	  (f.appIso (f ⁻¹ᵁ U)).symm ≪≫
717	    Y.presheaf.mapIso (eqToIso <| (f.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf U).symm).op
718	
719	@[simp]
720	lemma ΓIso_inv {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] (U : Y.Opens) :
721	    (ΓIso f U).inv = f.appLE (f.opensRange ⊓ U) (f ⁻¹ᵁ U)
722	      (by rw [← f.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf, f.preimage_image_eq]) := by
723	  simp only [ΓIso, Iso.trans_inv, Functor.mapIso_inv, Iso.op_inv, eqToIso.inv, eqToHom_op,
724	    Iso.symm_inv, Scheme.Hom.appIso_hom', Scheme.Hom.map_appLE]
725	
726	@[reassoc, elementwise]
727	lemma map_ΓIso_inv {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] (U : Y.Opens) :
728	    Y.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_right).op ≫ (ΓIso f U).inv = f.app U := by
729	  simp [Scheme.Hom.appLE_eq_app]
730	
731	@[reassoc, elementwise]
732	lemma app_ΓIso_hom {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsOpenImmersion f] (U : Y.Opens) :
733	    f.app U ≫ (ΓIso f U).hom = Y.presheaf.map (homOfLE inf_le_right).op := by
734	  rw [← map_ΓIso_inv]
735	  simp [-ΓIso_inv]

738	  of `U` and the sections of `X` at the image of `f`. -/

700	/-- The isomorphism between the fibred product of two schemes `Spec S` and `Spec T`
701	over a scheme `Spec R` and the `Spec` of the tensor product `S ⊗[R] T`. -/
702	noncomputable
703	def pullbackSpecIso :
704	    pullback (Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap R S)))
705	      (Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap R T))) ≅ Spec (.of <| S ⊗[R] T) :=
706	  letI H := IsLimit.equivIsoLimit (PullbackCone.eta _)
707	    (PushoutCocone.isColimitEquivIsLimitOp _ (CommRingCat.pushoutCoconeIsColimit R S T))
708	  limit.isoLimitCone ⟨_, isLimitPullbackConeMapOfIsLimit Scheme.Spec _ H⟩
709	
710	/--
711	The composition of the inverse of the isomorphism `pullbackSpecIso R S T` (from the pullback of
712	`Spec S ⟶ Spec R` and `Spec T ⟶ Spec R` to `Spec (S ⊗[R] T)`) with the first projection is
713	the morphism `Spec (S ⊗[R] T) ⟶ Spec S` obtained by applying `Spec.map` to the ring morphism
714	`s ↦ s ⊗ₜ[R] 1`.
715	-/
716	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
717	lemma pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst :
718	    (pullbackSpecIso R S T).inv ≫ pullback.fst _ _ = Spec.map (ofHom includeLeftRingHom) :=
719	  limit.isoLimitCone_inv_π _ _
720

722	lemma pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst' :
723	    (pullbackSpecIso R S T).inv ≫ pullback.fst _ _ = Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap S _)) :=
724	  pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst ..
725	
726	/--
727	The composition of the inverse of the isomorphism `pullbackSpecIso R S T` (from the pullback of
728	`Spec S ⟶ Spec R` and `Spec T ⟶ Spec R` to `Spec (S ⊗[R] T)`) with the second projection is
729	the morphism `Spec (S ⊗[R] T) ⟶ Spec T` obtained by applying `Spec.map` to the ring morphism
730	`t ↦ 1 ⊗ₜ[R] t`.
731	-/
732	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
733	lemma pullbackSpecIso_inv_snd :
734	    (pullbackSpecIso R S T).inv ≫ pullback.snd _ _ =
735	      Spec.map (ofHom (R := T) (S := S ⊗[R] T) (toRingHom includeRight)) :=
736	  limit.isoLimitCone_inv_π _ _
737	
738	/--
739	The composition of the isomorphism `pullbackSpecIso R S T` (from the pullback of
740	`Spec S ⟶ Spec R` and `Spec T ⟶ Spec R` to `Spec (S ⊗[R] T)`) with the morphism
741	`Spec (S ⊗[R] T) ⟶ Spec S` obtained by applying `Spec.map` to the ring morphism `s ↦ s ⊗ₜ[R] 1`
742	is the first projection.
743	-/
744	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
745	lemma pullbackSpecIso_hom_fst :
746	    (pullbackSpecIso R S T).hom ≫ Spec.map (ofHom includeLeftRingHom) = pullback.fst _ _ := by
747	  rw [← pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst, Iso.hom_inv_id_assoc]
748

751	    (pullbackSpecIso R S T).hom ≫ Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap S _)) = pullback.fst _ _ :=
752	  pullbackSpecIso_hom_fst ..

756	`Spec S ⟶ Spec R` and `Spec T ⟶ Spec R` to `Spec (S ⊗[R] T)`) with the morphism
757	`Spec (S ⊗[R] T) ⟶ Spec T` obtained by applying `Spec.map` to the ring morphism `t ↦ 1 ⊗ₜ[R] t`
758	is the second projection.

772	    (inl : B ⟶ P) (inr : C ⟶ P) (h : IsPushout f g inl inr) :

41	/-! ### Recipe 1 helpers (iter-184 Lane B, `analogies/gmscaling-projection-idiom.md`):
42	project-side simp helpers for `pullback.map ≫ pullback.fst/snd`.
43	
44	Mathlib's `pullback.lift_fst` / `pullback.lift_snd` are `@[reassoc]` ONLY, not
45	`@[simp]`, so the abbrev `pullback.map _ _ _ _ i₁ i₂ i₃ _ _ ≫ pullback.fst _ _`
46	is not collapsed by `simp` even though the equation holds definitionally. These
47	two local simp lemmas unblock projection-chain collapse for
48	`gmScalingP1_cover_intersection_X_iso` whose STEP 4 uses
49	`asIso (pullback.map …)`. Candidate for upstream Mathlib contribution. -/

53	    (f₁ : W ⟶ S) (f₂ : X ⟶ S) [Limits.HasPullback f₁ f₂] (g₁ : Y ⟶ T)
54	    (g₂ : Z ⟶ T) [Limits.HasPullback g₁ g₂] (i₁ : W ⟶ Y) (i₂ : X ⟶ Z) (i₃ : S ⟶ T)
55	    (eq₁ : f₁ ≫ i₃ = i₁ ≫ g₁) (eq₂ : f₂ ≫ i₃ = i₂ ≫ g₂) :
56	    Limits.pullback.map f₁ f₂ g₁ g₂ i₁ i₂ i₃ eq₁ eq₂ ≫
57	        Limits.pullback.fst g₁ g₂ =
58	      Limits.pullback.fst f₁ f₂ ≫ i₁ :=
59	  Limits.pullback.lift_fst _ _ _

64	    (g₂ : Z ⟶ T) [Limits.HasPullback g₁ g₂] (i₁ : W ⟶ Y) (i₂ : X ⟶ Z) (i₃ : S ⟶ T)

77	/-- **`Proj.awayι 𝒜 f _ _ ≫ PLB.hom = Spec.map (algebraMap kbar (Away 𝒜 f))`** for any
78	homogeneous element `f` of positive degree.

80	Generic in the element `f` AND in the degree `m` so we can apply it to either
81	`(![X 0, X 1]) i` (the actual chart input from `projectiveLineBarAffineCover.openCover.f i`,
82	which uses `m = (![1, 1]) i`) or `X i` (which uses `m = 1`). A pure rewrite chasing

84	private lemma awayι_comp_PLB_hom (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
85	    {m : ℕ} (hm : 0 < m)

87	    Proj.awayι (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) f hf hm ≫
88	      (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom =
89	    Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap kbar
90	      (HomogeneousLocalization.Away (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) f))) := by
91	  change Proj.awayι _ _ _ _ ≫ Proj.toSpecZero _ ≫ Spec.map _ = _
92	  rw [← Category.assoc, Proj.awayι_toSpecZero, ← Spec.map_comp,
93	    ← CommRingCat.ofHom_comp]
94	  rfl

101	
102	Chartwise definition: on `𝔸¹ × 𝔾_m` (target chart `D₊(X₀)` of `ℙ¹`), the morphism is
103	the polynomial map `(x, λ) ↦ λx`; near `∞` (target chart `D₊(X₁)`, coordinate `u = 1/x`),
104	the target coordinate `1/(λx) = u/λ` is regular because `λ ∈ 𝔾_m` is invertible. The two
105	chart-restrictions agree on `(𝔸¹ ∖ {0}) × 𝔾_m`, so they glue via
106	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms`.
107	
108	The companion lemma `gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero` exposes the load-bearing fixed-point
109	property `σ_×(0, λ) = 0` for all `λ ∈ 𝔾_m`, packaged as the `W`-axis-collapse hypothesis
110	that `hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity` (Cor 1.5) consumes. -/
111	
112	/-- **Chart-1 ring map for `σ_×`** at the `MvPolynomial Unit kbar`-level: sends the affine
113	coord `u = X 0 / X 1 ↦ u ⊗ λ`, where `λ = X () ∈ GmRing kbar`. Uses
114	`MvPolynomial.eval₂Hom` with the algebra-map `kbar →+* MvPolynomial Unit kbar ⊗[kbar] GmRing`
115	(target carrier carries `Algebra kbar` because both factors do). Axiom-clean. -/
116	noncomputable def gmScalingP1_chart1_ringMap (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] :
117	    MvPolynomial Unit kbar →+* TensorProduct kbar (MvPolynomial Unit kbar) (GmRing kbar) :=
118	  MvPolynomial.eval₂Hom (algebraMap kbar _)
119	    (fun _ => (MvPolynomial.X () : MvPolynomial Unit kbar) ⊗ₜ[kbar]
120	      (algebraMap (MvPolynomial Unit kbar) (GmRing kbar) (MvPolynomial.X ())))
121	
122	/-- **Chart-0 ring map for `σ_×`** at the `MvPolynomial Unit kbar`-level: sends the affine

321	/-- Given a family of homogeneous elements `f` of positive degree that spans the irrelevant ideal,
322	`Spec (A_f)₀ ⟶ Proj A` forms an affine open cover of `Proj A`. -/
323	noncomputable
324	def affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan {ι : Type*} (f : ι → A) {m : ι → ℕ}
325	    (f_deg : ∀ i, f i ∈ 𝒜 (m i)) (hm : ∀ i, 0 < m i)
326	    (hf : (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant 𝒜).toIdeal ≤ Ideal.span (Set.range f)) :
327	    (Proj 𝒜).AffineOpenCover where
328	  I₀ := ι
329	  X i := .of (Away 𝒜 (f i))
330	  f i := awayι 𝒜 (f i) (f_deg i) (hm i)
331	  idx x := (mem_iSup.mp ((iSup_basicOpen_eq_top 𝒜 f hf).ge (Set.mem_univ x))).choose
332	  covers x := by
333	    change x ∈ (awayι 𝒜 _ _ _).opensRange
334	    rw [opensRange_awayι]
335	    exact (mem_iSup.mp ((iSup_basicOpen_eq_top 𝒜 f hf).ge (Set.mem_univ x))).choose_spec
336	
337	/-- `Proj A` is covered by `Spec (A_f)₀` for all homogeneous elements of positive degree. -/
338	@[simps! f] noncomputable
339	def affineOpenCover : (Proj 𝒜).AffineOpenCover :=
340	  affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan 𝒜
341	    (ι := Σ i : PNat, 𝒜 i) (m := fun i ↦ i.1) (fun i ↦ i.2) (fun i ↦ i.2.2) (fun i ↦ i.1.2) <| by
342	  classical
343	  intro z hz
344	  rw [← DirectSum.sum_support_decompose 𝒜 z]
345	  refine Ideal.sum_mem _ fun c hc ↦ if hc0 : c = 0 then ?_ else
346	    Ideal.subset_span ⟨⟨⟨c, Nat.pos_iff_ne_zero.mpr hc0⟩, _⟩, rfl⟩
347	  convert Ideal.zero_mem _
348	  subst hc0
349	  exact hz
350	
351	end basicOpen

# AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean

## Lane E — `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion` (line 252 body, sorry at body)

### Iter-188 Attempt — 6-step appTop recipe test

- **Objective (per PROGRESS.md / iter-188 plan):** execute the 6-step appTop
  recipe (helper `r_1_appTop_isLocElem_eq_one` via cancel_mono on `Proj.awayι`
  + `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app`, then telescope simp through `comp_appTop`,
  `ΓSpecIso_naturality`, `pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst/snd`, `pullback.lift_fst/snd`,
  then discharge residual via the helper). HARD BAR ≥1 axiom-clean close or
  escalate to Mathlib analogy consult iter-189.
- **Result:** PARTIAL — 0 closure, HARD BAR fires; the simp telescope is
  applied as `simp only [Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, Scheme.Hom.appTop]` (line ~496
  in the updated body) which advances the residual goal from the raw
  `Scheme.Hom.appTop (LHS_big_composition) = Scheme.Hom.appTop (Spec.map alg)`
  form into the `.app ⊤` chain form (6-factor product). The trailing `sorry`
  is preserved.

### Attempt 1 — Default `simp` discharge tested

- **Approach:** `simp` (default set) on the line-432 residual.
- **Result:** PARTIAL — `simp` rewrites `comp_appTop`/`Iso.trans_hom`/etc. into
  a 6-factor `.app ⊤` chain but does not close the goal. The chain factors
  `(Spec.map eval₂Hom).app ⊤`, `(pullbackSpecIso).hom.app ⊤`,
  `(pullback.congrHom or pullback.map).app ⊤`,
  `(pullbackRightPullbackFstIso).hom.app ⊤`,
  `(pullbackSymmetry).hom.app ⊤`,
  `(pullback.lift (pullback.lift _ (Gm.hom ≫ r_1) _) ...).app ⊤`, and the RHS
  `(Spec.map (algMap MvPoly → GmRing)).app ⊤`.

### Attempt 2 — `cancel_mono` rewrite to explicit lift form

- **Approach:** rewrite `r_1 = IsOpenImmersion.lift (Proj.awayι _) onePt.left _`
  via `cancel_mono` on the open immersion `Proj.awayι (X 1)` and `h_r_1`. The
  intent was to gain access to `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app` (Mathlib:
  `OpenImmersion.lean:696`) for computing `r_1.appTop` on `isLocElem`.
- **Result:** FAILED in-session — the `Set.range _ ⊆ Set.range _` side-condition
  for the lift constructor requires the chart-1 factorization range
  containment proof, which is exactly the body of `iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor`
  (lines 106–163). Re-establishing it here would be code duplication. Cleaner
  path: refactor `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion`'s signature to
  accept the explicit lift (not the opaque r_1), OR add a separate helper for
  the range subset. Both are iter-189+ refactor moves.

### Attempt 3 — `MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext` after `CommRingCat.hom_ext`

- **Approach:** `ext1; apply MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext` to descend to per-generator
  ring-map equality (`C r` and `X ()` cases).
- **Result:** FAILED — `apply MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext` does not unify with
  the post-`.hom`-stripped goal shape (the LHS chain has many `.c.app` factors
  that don't directly expose the `MvPolynomial Unit kbar →+* GmRing kbar`
  ring map for `ringHom_ext` to fire on). Requires additional rewrites
  (Spec.preimage chain or `homEquiv` symmetric form) to expose the underlying
  ring map first.

### Key Insight — Why r_1 is opaque

The hypothesis `h_r_1 : r_1 ≫ Proj.awayι (X 1) _ _ = onePt.left` combined with
`Mono (Proj.awayι _)` (open immersion ⟹ mono) gives that `r_1` is uniquely
determined; specifically `r_1 = IsOpenImmersion.lift (Proj.awayι _) onePt.left
h_range` for the canonical range-subset proof. But computing `r_1.appTop`
abstractly from `h_r_1` alone is BLOCKED because:

- `(Proj.awayι _).appTop : Γ(Proj 𝒜, ⊤) → Γ(Spec(Away 𝒜 X_1), ⊤) = Away 𝒜 X_1`
  has image **= `kbar ⊂ Away 𝒜 X_1`** (the degree-0 part; `Γ(Proj 𝒜, ⊤) = kbar`).
- `isLocElem = [X_0/X_1]` is **NOT** in that image (it's the chart coordinate,
  not a scalar).
- So `h_r_1`-from-cancellation `(Proj.awayι).appTop ≫ r_1.appTop =
  onePt.left.appTop` (post-`comp_appTop` rewrite) cannot evaluate
  `r_1.appTop(isLocElem)` directly.

The substantive computation requires `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app` applied to
`f = Proj.awayι (X 1)`, `V = ⊤`:
```
(IsOpenImmersion.lift f g H).app V
  = (f.appIso V).inv ≫ g.app (f ''ᵁ V) ≫ presheaf.map (eqToHom _).op
```
Specifically `(Proj.awayι _).appIso ⊤ : Γ(Spec(Away 𝒜 X_1), ⊤) ≅ Γ(Proj 𝒜, D₊(X_1))`
(since `awayι ⁻¹ᵁ ⊤ = ⊤` and `awayι ''ᵁ ⊤` lies in `awayι.opensRange = D₊(X_1)`).

The `appIso` hom maps `isLocElem ↦ [X_0/X_1] ∈ Γ(Proj 𝒜, D₊(X_1))`, and
`onePt.left.app D₊(X_1)` applied to `[X_0/X_1]` evaluates to `v(X_0)/v(X_1)`
with `v = (1, 1)`, giving `1`. This is the `Proj.appIso` / per-open
evaluation machinery exceeded the iter-188 attempt budget.

### Dead End Warnings (for iter-189)

- DO NOT attempt to compute `r_1.appTop` from `h_r_1` alone — the
  image-mismatch on `(Proj.awayι).appTop` makes this structurally impossible.
- DO NOT attempt `Spec.preimage` on `r_1` to "get the underlying ring map"
  and then evaluate it — same blocker; `r_1` is opaque modulo its lift
  characterization, and `Spec.preimage_comp` only gives the composite ring map,
  not the individual factor.

### Iter-189 Closure Plan (per HARD BAR escalation to Mathlib analogy)

1. **Mathlib analogist call:** "Evaluate `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app` of
   `Proj.awayι` composed with a `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` source on the
   basic open `D₊(X_1) ⊆ Proj 𝒜`. Specifically: given `r_1 = IsOpenImmersion.lift
   (Proj.awayι (X 1)) onePt.left h_range`, compute `r_1.appTop ((ΓSpecIso _).inv
   isLocElem) = (ΓSpecIso k̄).inv 1`."
2. **Project-side helper** (if analogist returns viable recipe): build helper
   ```
   private lemma r_1_appTop_isLocElem_eq_one
       (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
       (r_1 : Spec k̄ ⟶ Spec(Away 𝒜 X_1))
       (h_r_1 : r_1 ≫ Proj.awayι _ _ _ = onePt.left) :
       r_1.appTop (isLocElem) = 1 := …
   ```
   Estimated ~30–50 LOC (likely more — depends on `Proj.appIso` accessibility).
3. **Body refactor:** after Step 2, the 6-step recipe in
   `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion`'s body reduces to a mechanical
   `simp [comp_appTop, ΓSpecIso_naturality, pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst, ...,
   r_1_appTop_isLocElem_eq_one]` chain + `MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext` constants
   close.

### Status Summary

- **Sorry count this file:** 2 (unchanged from entry).
  - Line 252 body (`iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion`): residual now
    in `.app ⊤` chain form post-simp-telescope.
  - Line 753 (`genusZero_curve_iso_P1`): RR-gated, deferred.
- **Axioms:** Carries propagated `sorryAx` only; no project axioms.
- **HARD BAR:** FIRES (0 axiom-clean close). Escalate to Mathlib analogy
  consult iter-189 per progress-critic must-fix rule.
- **Blueprint marker readiness:** No marker changes for this file
  (`thm:rigidity_lemma` etc. are in `RigidityLemma.lean`, not this file;
  `prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant` corresponds to
  `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` which is sorry-free in its own body but
  inherits `sorryAx` from the helper chain). Recommend review agent leave
  `\leanok` status to the deterministic sync_leanok pass.

### Code Comments Updated

The Stage 8 comment block (lines ~432–497 post-edit) was rewritten to reflect
the iter-188 attempt status, explicit image-mismatch blocker analysis, and
iter-189 analogist call brief. The `simp only [Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop,
Scheme.Hom.appTop]` step is applied before the residual sorry so the goal is
in a normalized chain form for iter-189 attack.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:

9	# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
15	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
16	consumes.
17	
18	Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
19	startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
20	geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
21	pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
22	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
23	`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
24	`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
25	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
26	Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.
27	
28	## Status (iter-178 update — projectiveDimension closed)
29	
30	This file was originally landed iter-175 (Lane F file-skeleton) with each
31	blueprint-pinned declaration carrying the *intended* substantive type
32	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
33	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex`). The bodies were
34	scheduled for iter-176+ work; the chapter is independent of the rest of
35	Route A's geometric infrastructure, making it a good parallel-work
36	candidate.
37	
38	iter-178 closed `Module.projectiveDimension` kernel-clean as a one-liner
39	re-export of the categorical `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension`. The
40	remaining five typed `sorry` bodies (`depth`, `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`,
41	`depth_of_short_exact`, `auslander_buchsbaum_formula`,
42	`CohenMacaulay.of_regular`) are substantive multi-iter content and stay
43	gated on dedicated body lanes.
44	
45	The 7 pinned declarations are:
46	
47	1. `RingTheory.Module.depth` (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the `I`-depth of a
48	   finite `R`-module as the supremum of lengths of `M`-regular sequences in
49	   `I`, valued in `ℕ∞`.
50	2. `RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — for
51	   `(R, 𝔪)` Noetherian local and `M ≠ 0` finite, `depth(M)` equals the
52	   smallest `i` with `Ext^i_R(κ, M) ≠ 0`. Encoded via the depth-bound iff
53	   `Ext` vanishes below.
54	3. `Module.projectiveDimension` (noncomputable def, ~3 LOC) — an
55	   `R`-module-side wrapper for the categorical
56	   `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R M)`. The categorical
57	   version exists in Mathlib `b80f227`; this is the re-export pinned by the
58	   blueprint.
59	4. `RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact` (theorem, ~12 LOC) — the
60	   depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequalities (Stacks 00LE).
61	5. `RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the formula
62	   `pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)` for a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite
63	   projective dimension over a Noetherian local ring.
64	6. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay` (class, ~3 LOC) — `IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing R`
65	   encoded as `depth(R) = krullDim R`.
66	7. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular` (theorem, ~6 LOC) — a regular local
67	   ring is Cohen–Macaulay (the consumer-facing input for A.4.a).
68	
69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
72	declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	- `depth` returns `ℕ∞` and is defined by the regular-sequence supremum.
75	- `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` is encoded as the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`
76	  vanishing characterisation (the smallest non-vanishing `Ext` index = depth).
77	- `projectiveDimension` re-exports the categorical
78	  `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on `ModuleCat.of R M`.
79	- `depth_of_short_exact` packages the three Stacks 00LE inequalities into a
80	  conjunction.
81	- `auslander_buchsbaum_formula` is the numeric equation
82	  `pd + depth = depth(R)`.
83	- `CohenMacaulay` is the equation `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`.
84	- `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` is an `IsRegularLocalRing → IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing`
85	  implication, the consumer-facing statement.
86	
87	Unfolding any declaration exposes the named substantive content (a regular-
88	sequence supremum, an `Ext`-vanishing characterisation, the categorical
89	projective dimension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` /
90	empty-content `proof_wanted` placeholders are used.
91	
92	## References
93	
94	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex` (~560 LOC,
95	7 pins). Stacks Project tags 00LF (definition-depth), 00LP (lemma-depth-ext),
96	00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses), 090V (proposition-Auslander–Buchsbaum), 00N4
97	(definition-local-ring-CM), 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM). Matsumura,
98	*Commutative Ring Theory*, Theorem 19.1. Auslander–Buchsbaum, "Homological
99	dimension in local rings", 1957.
100	-/

112	/-! ## §1. Depth of a finite module
113	
114	The `I`-depth of a finite `R`-module `M` is the supremum in
115	`{0, 1, 2, …, ∞}` of the lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in `I`
116	(provided `IM ≠ M`; if `IM = M` we set `depth_I(M) = ∞`). Mathlib `b80f227`
117	exposes the regular-sequence predicate `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`
118	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) but not the resulting
119	numeric depth function — that is the gap this declaration fills.
120	
121	Blueprint reference: `def:depth` (Stacks tag 00LF). -/
122	
123	/-- The **`I`-depth** of a finite `R`-module `M`: the supremum (in `ℕ∞`) of
124	lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in the ideal `I`.
125	
126	When `IM = M` (the "trivial-quotient" case, e.g. `M = 0` or `I = R`) the
127	supremum is taken to be `⊤` by convention. When `(R, 𝔪)` is local one usually
128	calls `depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M` simply *the depth* of `M`.
129	
130	iter-176+: the body is the supremum
131	```
132	sSup { (n : ℕ∞) | ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ I) ∧
133	                  RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs }
134	```
135	folded with the `IM = M` clause. The signature is non-tautological: it
136	asserts a function `(Ideal R) → (M : Type v) → ℕ∞` matching the Stacks
137	00LF definition.
138	
139	**iter-179 Mathlib-gap check (Lane F Target 2 STRETCH)**: a pinned-commit
140	audit of `Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.Depth` (the only file under
141	`Mathlib/RingTheory/*` containing the word "depth") confirms that Mathlib
142	`b80f227` ships only depth-zero lemmas (`IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff`)
143	and *not* the numeric depth function itself. The one-liner re-export route
144	is therefore not available; the body stays a typed `sorry` until an
145	iter-180+ body lane fills the supremum-with-`IM=M` clause directly. -/
146	noncomputable def depth {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (_I : Ideal R)
147	    (_M : Type v) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : ℕ∞ :=
148	  open Classical in
149	  if _I • (⊤ : Submodule R _M) = ⊤ then (⊤ : ℕ∞)
150	  else sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
151	    (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ _I) ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular _M rs }
152	
153	end Module
154	
155	end RingTheory

157	/-! ## §2. Projective dimension
158	
159	Mathlib `b80f227` exposes the categorical
160	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension : C → WithBot ℕ∞` on an abelian category
161	with enough projectives (file `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension`).
162	For `R`-modules this specialises to `ModuleCat.of R M`. The blueprint pins the
163	`Module.projectiveDimension` name as the re-export that downstream consumers
164	can use directly on an `R`-module without first packaging it in `ModuleCat`.
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:projective_dimension`. -/
167	
168	namespace Module
169	
170	/-- The **projective dimension** of an `R`-module `M`, defined as the
171	categorical projective dimension of `ModuleCat.of R M`.
172	
173	This is a re-export of `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` specialised to
174	the abelian category `ModuleCat R`. The categorical definition is the
175	infimum (in `WithBot ℕ∞`) of `n : ℕ` such that all `Ext^i(M, -)` vanish for
176	`i > n`, equivalently the smallest length of a projective resolution of `M`.
177	
178	Mathlib has the categorical `projectiveDimension` and the module-specific
179	`ModuleCat.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv`; the blueprint pins the
180	wrapper name so downstream consumers can write
181	`Module.projectiveDimension R M` rather than threading `ModuleCat.of`.
182	
183	The body is the one-line re-export
184	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)`; iter-178 closed
185	this declaration kernel-clean. -/
186	noncomputable def projectiveDimension (R : Type u) [Ring R]
187	    (_M : Type u) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
188	  CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)
189	
190	end Module
191	
192	namespace RingTheory
193	
194	namespace Module
